Build a Telegram Mini App game called Orbit Dash.

It should be a simple 3D space runner game where the player controls a small spaceship, dodges asteroids, collects coins, and tries to survive as long as possible.

The game should work smoothly inside Telegram Mini App WebView.

Include:
- Start screen
- 3D spaceship movement
- Asteroids as obstacles
- Coin collection
- Score counter
- Game over screen
- Restart button
- Mobile touch controls

planMarkdown:
Bot: Orbit Dash Telegram Mini App Production Plan

Summary:
Orbit Dash is a Telegram Mini App game where players pilot a small spaceship through a 3D space tunnel, dodge asteroids, collect coins, and compete for survival time and high scores.

The production system consists of a single Node.js ESM service using grammY for Telegram bot interactions, serving the Mini App frontend, exposing lightweight game APIs, validating Telegram Mini App identity data, and persisting player progress and scores in MongoDB. The game itself runs inside the Telegram Mini App WebView and is optimized for mobile touch controls, fast loading, and stable frame rates.

Product goals:
Orbit Dash should feel like a lightweight arcade game that opens instantly from Telegram and is simple to understand within a few seconds.

1) Core gameplay: the player controls a spaceship moving left, right, up, and down while the world scrolls forward. Asteroids appear as obstacles, coins appear as collectibles, and survival time increases difficulty.

2) Required screens: the Mini App includes a start screen, gameplay screen, pause or focus-loss handling, game over screen, score summary, coin summary, and restart button.

3) Telegram-native experience: the bot provides a clear entry point through commands and buttons, while the Mini App uses Telegram user identity for profiles, leaderboard entries, and score submission.

4) Competitive loop: players can replay quickly, improve their score, collect coins, and compare scores through daily, weekly, and all-time leaderboards.

5) Production readiness: the system should include identity validation, score plausibility checks, rate limiting, error handling, privacy-aware data storage, asset optimization, and operational visibility through structured logs and basic health checks.

High-level architecture:
The system runs as one Node.js service/process. It has three responsibilities in the same service: Telegram bot handling through grammY, Mini App delivery, and game backend APIs.

1) Telegram bot layer: grammY receives updates from Telegram and handles commands such as start, play, leaderboard, profile, help, and privacy. The bot sends Mini App launch buttons that open Orbit Dash inside Telegram WebView. The Telegram bot token is read from the TELEGRAMBOTTOKEN environment variable.

2) Mini App frontend: the browser game is delivered as static frontend assets from the Node.js service or from simple filesystem storage. It contains the start screen, 3D renderer, touch controls, collision detection, scoring HUD, game over screen, and restart loop. The rendering and gameplay loop run locally in the WebView to avoid network dependency during active play.

3) Backend API layer: the Mini App calls backend endpoints to initialize the user session, fetch player profile, fetch leaderboard data, submit game results, and update non-sensitive preferences. Every Mini App API request that depends on identity must validate Telegram Mini App init data server-side before trusting the Telegram user id.

4) Persistence layer: MongoDB stores users, game runs, leaderboard records, coin balances, and audit metadata. In-memory maps may be used only for optional short-lived concerns such as per-process rate limits, replay submission cooldowns, and temporary health counters.

5) Game client structure: the game loop separates rendering, input, spawning, physics, collision, scoring, and UI state. The client should use deterministic limits for maximum movement speed, asteroid spawn rate, coin spawn rate, and score growth so the backend can reject impossible submissions.

6) Asset handling: spaceship, asteroid, coin, background, sound, and texture assets are compressed and versioned. They may be served from simple filesystem storage or generic object storage. The first playable experience should load with a small critical asset set, with optional assets loaded after the start screen.

7) Deployment shape: a single Node.js server in the cloud exposes HTTPS endpoints for Telegram webhooks, Mini App pages, static assets, health checks, and game APIs. The service should support graceful shutdown, environment-based configuration, and clear separation between public Mini App routes and internal operational routes.

Data model:
MongoDB is the primary persistence store. The schema should remain simple and optimized for user lookup, recent runs, and leaderboard queries.

1) users: stores Telegram user identity and gameplay profile. Suggested fields include telegramUserId, username, firstName, languageCode, createdAt, lastSeenAt, totalRuns, bestScore, bestSurvivalMs, totalCoinsCollected, spendableCoins, settings, and status.

2) gameRuns: stores each completed attempt. Suggested fields include runId, telegramUserId, startedAt, endedAt, durationMs, score, coinsCollected, asteroidsDodged, maxDifficultyLevel, clientVersion, validationStatus, rejectionReason, deviceHints, and createdAt.

3) leaderboardEntries: stores denormalized best results for fast leaderboard display. Suggested fields include scope, periodKey, telegramUserId, displayName, score, survivalMs, coinsCollected, runId, rankUpdatedAt, and createdAt. Scopes can include daily, weekly, and allTime.

4) coinLedger: optional but recommended if coins will later unlock skins or upgrades. Suggested fields include ledgerId, telegramUserId, amount, reason, sourceRunId, balanceAfter, createdAt, and metadata. For a simple first release, coin totals can be stored directly on users and runs, but a ledger is safer for future economy features.

5) userSessions or launchEvents: optional analytics collection for Mini App opens. Suggested fields include telegramUserId, launchAt, platform, appVersion, referrerCommand, and initValidationStatus.

6) adminEvents: optional collection for moderation or operational actions, such as resetting suspicious scores, banning abusive users, or changing leaderboard visibility.

Important indexes include unique telegramUserId on users, telegramUserId plus createdAt on gameRuns, scope plus periodKey plus score descending on leaderboardEntries, and telegramUserId plus scope plus periodKey on leaderboardEntries.

Key flows:
1) First launch flow: the user sends /start or taps a shared bot link. The bot replies with a short explanation of Orbit Dash and a Play Orbit Dash button. When tapped, Telegram opens the Mini App WebView. The Mini App sends Telegram init data to the backend, the backend validates it, creates or updates the user, and returns profile data plus current leaderboard summary.

2) Start screen flow: the Mini App shows the game title, best score, total coins, a Play button, a leaderboard shortcut, and basic control instructions. Assets needed for the first run are preloaded before enabling Play.

3) Gameplay flow: after tapping Play, the spaceship appears in a 3D space lane. The player uses mobile touch controls, such as drag-to-move or virtual joystick movement. Asteroids spawn ahead and move toward the player. Coins spawn in reachable paths. The score increases with survival time, coins collected, and asteroids dodged. Difficulty increases gradually through speed, obstacle density, and coin placement risk.

4) Collision and collection flow: the client detects spaceship collisions with asteroids and coins. Asteroid collision ends the run. Coin collision increments the coin counter and may provide immediate feedback through animation and sound. The HUD continuously displays score, coins, and survival time.

5) Game over flow: when a collision occurs, the client stops the game loop, shows final score, survival time, coins collected, and whether a new personal best was achieved. The client submits the run result to the backend. The backend validates plausibility, stores the run, updates the user profile, updates leaderboard entries if eligible, and returns the accepted result plus rank information.

6) Restart flow: the Restart button resets local game state without leaving the Mini App. The user can replay immediately. The backend does not need to create a server-side run at the moment gameplay starts unless anti-abuse requirements increase; for launch, validating submitted completed runs is sufficient.

7) Leaderboard flow: users can open leaderboards from the bot command or Mini App. The backend returns top daily, weekly, and all-time scores, plus the current user’s rank if available. Display names should be derived from Telegram profile data but sanitized and length-limited.

8) Anti-cheat flow: the backend rejects impossible run submissions based on duration, maximum theoretical score, maximum coin count, movement limits, game version, duplicate run ids, and submission frequency. Suspicious but not impossible runs can be stored with a flagged validation status and excluded from leaderboards pending review.

9) Returning user flow: when a known player opens the Mini App, the backend updates lastSeenAt and returns saved profile data, best score, coin balance, and latest leaderboard rank. The game should remain playable even if leaderboard fetching fails, but score submission requires a valid backend response.

10) Error flow: if Mini App identity validation fails, the game shows a friendly message asking the user to reopen from Telegram. If the backend is temporarily unavailable, the client should allow a local run but clearly indicate that leaderboard submission may not be saved.

Tech stack:
The production stack is intentionally simple and aligned with the single-process requirement.

1) Runtime: Node.js using ESM modules.

2) Telegram framework: grammY for bot commands, launch buttons, update handling, and Telegram integration. TELEGRAMBOTTOKEN is loaded from environment variables.

3) Mini App: Telegram Mini App WebView running a modern browser-based 3D game frontend. The frontend should use a mobile-friendly 3D rendering library or direct browser graphics APIs, with careful asset compression and frame-rate optimization.

4) Backend HTTP layer: the same Node.js service serves Mini App files and exposes game APIs for initialization, profile retrieval, leaderboard retrieval, and score submission.

5) Persistence: MongoDB for users, game runs, leaderboards, coin balances, and audit data. Simple in-memory maps may be used for optional short-lived rate limiting or cooldowns, understanding that they reset when the process restarts.

6) File and image storage: game assets can be served from simple filesystem storage or generic object storage. No separate database is required for assets.

7) Deployment: a single Node.js server in the cloud with HTTPS support. The service should expose Telegram webhook handling, Mini App routes, API routes, and health checks from the same process.

8) Configuration: environment variables for bot token, public Mini App URL, MongoDB connection string, runtime environment, logging level, and score validation thresholds.

9) Observability: structured application logs, request ids, startup checks, health endpoint, and error reporting through logs. Keep operational dependencies minimal and avoid external queues or additional infrastructure.

Non-functional requirements:
1) Performance: the Mini App should target smooth gameplay on modern mobile devices. Keep initial load small, compress 3D assets, avoid excessive draw calls, reuse objects during gameplay, and avoid memory growth across restarts.

2) Reliability: the game loop should not depend on continuous network access. Backend calls happen at initialization, leaderboard loading, and score submission. Bot command handling and Mini App APIs should fail gracefully with user-friendly messages.

3) Security: validate Telegram Mini App init data on the server before trusting identity. Never accept user ids directly from the client as proof of identity. Apply input validation, output sanitization, request size limits, duplicate run protection, and score plausibility checks.

4) Privacy: store only the Telegram identity fields needed for gameplay and leaderboards. Provide a privacy command explaining what is stored. Consider a user status field for deleted, banned, or hidden-from-leaderboard accounts.

5) Abuse prevention: rate limit score submissions per user in memory, reject impossible scores, cap coins per run, track suspicious client versions, and allow manual invalidation of leaderboard entries through data updates.

6) Maintainability: separate concerns between bot handlers, Mini App API handlers, game domain services, validation rules, persistence adapters, and static asset delivery. Keep gameplay constants versioned so score validation can match the client version.

7) Compatibility: test inside Telegram Mini App WebView on common mobile screen sizes and both touch interaction styles: drag movement and virtual joystick. Handle safe areas, orientation changes, app backgrounding, and WebView focus loss.

8) Scalability within constraints: MongoDB indexes should support leaderboard reads and user lookups efficiently. Since the service is single-process, long-running work should be avoided in request handlers, and the game client should do all rendering and physics locally.

9) Release management: use appVersion and gameRulesVersion in client initialization and score submission. This allows the backend to validate old runs correctly during gradual frontend updates.

Bot commands:
• start — Welcomes the user, explains Orbit Dash briefly, and presents the main Play Orbit Dash button that opens the Telegram Mini App. (usage: /start) [onboarding]
• play — Opens the Orbit Dash Mini App directly. This is the fastest path for returning players who want to start a new run. (usage: /play) [game]
• leaderboard — Shows a compact leaderboard summary in chat and provides a button to open the full leaderboard inside the Mini App. (usage: /leaderboard) [competition]
• profile — Shows the player’s best score, total runs, total coins collected, current coin balance, and recent rank summary. (usage: /profile) [account]
• help — Explains the controls, objective, scoring rules, coins, asteroids, restart behavior, and how leaderboard rankings work. (usage: /help) [support]
• settings — Provides access to simple player preferences such as sound, haptics if supported, control style, and leaderboard display visibility. (usage: /settings) [account]
• privacy — Explains what Telegram profile data and gameplay data are stored, why they are needed, and how they are used for scores and leaderboards. (usage: /privacy) [support]

DOCS.md.content:
# Orbit Dash Documentation

Orbit Dash is a Telegram-only Mini App game. Players pilot a small spaceship through a space runner course, dodge asteroids, collect coins, and survive as long as possible.

The project runs as a single Node.js service. It starts a grammY Telegram bot and hosts the Mini App web game from the same process at `/app`.

## Public commands

### /start

Usage: `/start`

Welcomes the user to Orbit Dash and shows a Telegram Web App button that opens the game.

### /help

Usage: `/help`

Explains how to play: move the spaceship with touch controls, dodge asteroids, collect coins, survive longer for more score, and restart after game over.

## Environment variables

### TELEGRAM_BOT_TOKEN

Required. The Telegram bot token from BotFather. The service exits with a clear message if this is missing.

### WEBAPP_URL

Optional. The public URL that Telegram should open for the game, normally ending in `/app`.

Example:

text
https://your-service.onrender.com/app


If it is missing, the app falls back to `http://localhost:<PORT>/app` for local development. This keeps startup safe, but Telegram mobile users need a public HTTPS URL.

### PORT

Optional. HTTP port for the single Node service. Defaults to `3000`.

## Local run

Install root dependencies and web app dependencies:

bash
npm run install:root
npm run install:webapp


Build the Mini App:

bash
npm run build:webapp


Start the service:

bash
npm start


Open the local game at:

text
http://localhost:3000/app


## Telegram Mini App URL setup

For Telegram mobile testing, deploy the service to a public HTTPS URL and set:

text
WEBAPP_URL=https://your-public-host/app


Then send `/start` to the bot and tap Play Orbit Dash.

## Gameplay

The game includes a start screen, live game screen, and game over screen.

During a run:

1. Move the spaceship with drag or the on-screen controls.
2. Avoid asteroids. Hitting one ends the run.
3. Collect coins for bonus points.
4. Survive longer to increase score and difficulty.
5. Press Restart after game over to reset the run without leaving Telegram.

Desktop testing supports arrow keys and WASD.

## Deployment notes

Use one Node web service. Do not run a separate worker. The root `start` script runs the HTTP server and the Telegram bot together.

Recommended Render settings:

Build command:

bash
npm run build


Start command:

bash
npm start


Required environment variable:

text
TELEGRAM_BOT_TOKEN


Recommended environment variable:

text
WEBAPP_URL=https://your-service.onrender.com/app


## Troubleshooting

If the service exits, check that `TELEGRAM_BOT_TOKEN` is set.

If Telegram says the Web App cannot open, confirm `WEBAPP_URL` is public, HTTPS, and ends with `/app`.

If the game shows a WebGL error, update Telegram, try a newer mobile device, or test in a browser that supports WebGL.

If the web page says the app is not built, run `npm run build:webapp` before `npm start`.

Logs print only safe booleans such as whether `TELEGRAM_BOT_TOKEN` and `WEBAPP_URL` are set. Secrets are never printed.

README.md.content:
# Orbit Dash

Orbit Dash is a Telegram Mini App game served by one Node.js service. The same process runs the grammY Telegram bot, hosts the React and Phaser web game at `/app`, and exposes a small health endpoint.

## Features

- Telegram bot commands: `/start` and `/help`
- Telegram Web App button that opens Orbit Dash
- Mobile-first Telegram WebView UI
- Phaser 3 arcade loop with spaceship movement, asteroids, coins, score, survival timer, difficulty progression, and restart flow
- Local high score and total coin persistence in the browser
- Production-safe startup logs that only print environment booleans

## Setup

1. Install dependencies:

bash
npm run install:root
npm run install:webapp


2. Copy `.env.sample` to `.env` and set `TELEGRAM_BOT_TOKEN`.

3. Build the web app:

bash
npm run build:webapp


4. Start the single service:

bash
npm start


The game is served at `http://localhost:3000/app` by default.

## Environment variables

- `TELEGRAM_BOT_TOKEN`: required. Telegram bot token from BotFather.
- `WEBAPP_URL`: optional but recommended. Full public URL to the game, for example `https://your-service.onrender.com/app`.
- `PORT`: optional. HTTP port for the Node service. Defaults to `3000`.

## Commands

- `/start`: welcomes the player and shows the Play Orbit Dash Web App button.
- `/help`: explains the objective, touch controls, scoring, coins, asteroids, and restart flow.

## Deployment

Deploy as one Render web service or similar Node host.

Build command:

bash
npm run build


Start command:

bash
npm start


Set `TELEGRAM_BOT_TOKEN`. Set `WEBAPP_URL` to your deployed `/app` URL when available.

## Troubleshooting

If the bot exits at startup, confirm `TELEGRAM_BOT_TOKEN` is set. Logs only show whether the token is present, never the token value.

If the button opens a local URL inside Telegram, set `WEBAPP_URL` to a public HTTPS URL ending in `/app`.

If the game shows a WebGL error, update Telegram, try another device, or confirm the WebView supports WebGL.

state.planMarkdown:
Bot: Orbit Dash Telegram Mini App Production Plan

Summary:
Orbit Dash is a Telegram Mini App game where players pilot a small spaceship through a 3D space tunnel, dodge asteroids, collect coins, and compete for survival time and high scores.

The production system consists of a single Node.js ESM service using grammY for Telegram bot interactions, serving the Mini App frontend, exposing lightweight game APIs, validating Telegram Mini App identity data, and persisting player progress and scores in MongoDB. The game itself runs inside the Telegram Mini App WebView and is optimized for mobile touch controls, fast loading, and stable frame rates.

Product goals:
Orbit Dash should feel like a lightweight arcade game that opens instantly from Telegram and is simple to understand within a few seconds.

1) Core gameplay: the player controls a spaceship moving left, right, up, and down while the world scrolls forward. Asteroids appear as obstacles, coins appear as collectibles, and survival time increases difficulty.

2) Required screens: the Mini App includes a start screen, gameplay screen, pause or focus-loss handling, game over screen, score summary, coin summary, and restart button.

3) Telegram-native experience: the bot provides a clear entry point through commands and buttons, while the Mini App uses Telegram user identity for profiles, leaderboard entries, and score submission.

4) Competitive loop: players can replay quickly, improve their score, collect coins, and compare scores through daily, weekly, and all-time leaderboards.

5) Production readiness: the system should include identity validation, score plausibility checks, rate limiting, error handling, privacy-aware data storage, asset optimization, and operational visibility through structured logs and basic health checks.

High-level architecture:
The system runs as one Node.js service/process. It has three responsibilities in the same service: Telegram bot handling through grammY, Mini App delivery, and game backend APIs.

1) Telegram bot layer: grammY receives updates from Telegram and handles commands such as start, play, leaderboard, profile, help, and privacy. The bot sends Mini App launch buttons that open Orbit Dash inside Telegram WebView. The Telegram bot token is read from the TELEGRAMBOTTOKEN environment variable.

2) Mini App frontend: the browser game is delivered as static frontend assets from the Node.js service or from simple filesystem storage. It contains the start screen, 3D renderer, touch controls, collision detection, scoring HUD, game over screen, and restart loop. The rendering and gameplay loop run locally in the WebView to avoid network dependency during active play.

3) Backend API layer: the Mini App calls backend endpoints to initialize the user session, fetch player profile, fetch leaderboard data, submit game results, and update non-sensitive preferences. Every Mini App API request that depends on identity must validate Telegram Mini App init data server-side before trusting the Telegram user id.

4) Persistence layer: MongoDB stores users, game runs, leaderboard records, coin balances, and audit metadata. In-memory maps may be used only for optional short-lived concerns such as per-process rate limits, replay submission cooldowns, and temporary health counters.

5) Game client structure: the game loop separates rendering, input, spawning, physics, collision, scoring, and UI state. The client should use deterministic limits for maximum movement speed, asteroid spawn rate, coin spawn rate, and score growth so the backend can reject impossible submissions.

6) Asset handling: spaceship, asteroid, coin, background, sound, and texture assets are compressed and versioned. They may be served from simple filesystem storage or generic object storage. The first playable experience should load with a small critical asset set, with optional assets loaded after the start screen.

7) Deployment shape: a single Node.js server in the cloud exposes HTTPS endpoints for Telegram webhooks, Mini App pages, static assets, health checks, and game APIs. The service should support graceful shutdown, environment-based configuration, and clear separation between public Mini App routes and internal operational routes.

Data model:
MongoDB is the primary persistence store. The schema should remain simple and optimized for user lookup, recent runs, and leaderboard queries.

1) users: stores Telegram user identity and gameplay profile. Suggested fields include telegramUserId, username, firstName, languageCode, createdAt, lastSeenAt, totalRuns, bestScore, bestSurvivalMs, totalCoinsCollected, spendableCoins, settings, and status.

2) gameRuns: stores each completed attempt. Suggested fields include runId, telegramUserId, startedAt, endedAt, durationMs, score, coinsCollected, asteroidsDodged, maxDifficultyLevel, clientVersion, validationStatus, rejectionReason, deviceHints, and createdAt.

3) leaderboardEntries: stores denormalized best results for fast leaderboard display. Suggested fields include scope, periodKey, telegramUserId, displayName, score, survivalMs, coinsCollected, runId, rankUpdatedAt, and createdAt. Scopes can include daily, weekly, and allTime.

4) coinLedger: optional but recommended if coins will later unlock skins or upgrades. Suggested fields include ledgerId, telegramUserId, amount, reason, sourceRunId, balanceAfter, createdAt, and metadata. For a simple first release, coin totals can be stored directly on users and runs, but a ledger is safer for future economy features.

5) userSessions or launchEvents: optional analytics collection for Mini App opens. Suggested fields include telegramUserId, launchAt, platform, appVersion, referrerCommand, and initValidationStatus.

6) adminEvents: optional collection for moderation or operational actions, such as resetting suspicious scores, banning abusive users, or changing leaderboard visibility.

Important indexes include unique telegramUserId on users, telegramUserId plus createdAt on gameRuns, scope plus periodKey plus score descending on leaderboardEntries, and telegramUserId plus scope plus periodKey on leaderboardEntries.

Key flows:
1) First launch flow: the user sends /start or taps a shared bot link. The bot replies with a short explanation of Orbit Dash and a Play Orbit Dash button. When tapped, Telegram opens the Mini App WebView. The Mini App sends Telegram init data to the backend, the backend validates it, creates or updates the user, and returns profile data plus current leaderboard summary.

2) Start screen flow: the Mini App shows the game title, best score, total coins, a Play button, a leaderboard shortcut, and basic control instructions. Assets needed for the first run are preloaded before enabling Play.

3) Gameplay flow: after tapping Play, the spaceship appears in a 3D space lane. The player uses mobile touch controls, such as drag-to-move or virtual joystick movement. Asteroids spawn ahead and move toward the player. Coins spawn in reachable paths. The score increases with survival time, coins collected, and asteroids dodged. Difficulty increases gradually through speed, obstacle density, and coin placement risk.

4) Collision and collection flow: the client detects spaceship collisions with asteroids and coins. Asteroid collision ends the run. Coin collision increments the coin counter and may provide immediate feedback through animation and sound. The HUD continuously displays score, coins, and survival time.

5) Game over flow: when a collision occurs, the client stops the game loop, shows final score, survival time, coins collected, and whether a new personal best was achieved. The client submits the run result to the backend. The backend validates plausibility, stores the run, updates the user profile, updates leaderboard entries if eligible, and returns the accepted result plus rank information.

6) Restart flow: the Restart button resets local game state without leaving the Mini App. The user can replay immediately. The backend does not need to create a server-side run at the moment gameplay starts unless anti-abuse requirements increase; for launch, validating submitted completed runs is sufficient.

7) Leaderboard flow: users can open leaderboards from the bot command or Mini App. The backend returns top daily, weekly, and all-time scores, plus the current user’s rank if available. Display names should be derived from Telegram profile data but sanitized and length-limited.

8) Anti-cheat flow: the backend rejects impossible run submissions based on duration, maximum theoretical score, maximum coin count, movement limits, game version, duplicate run ids, and submission frequency. Suspicious but not impossible runs can be stored with a flagged validation status and excluded from leaderboards pending review.

9) Returning user flow: when a known player opens the Mini App, the backend updates lastSeenAt and returns saved profile data, best score, coin balance, and latest leaderboard rank. The game should remain playable even if leaderboard fetching fails, but score submission requires a valid backend response.

10) Error flow: if Mini App identity validation fails, the game shows a friendly message asking the user to reopen from Telegram. If the backend is temporarily unavailable, the client should allow a local run but clearly indicate that leaderboard submission may not be saved.

Tech stack:
The production stack is intentionally simple and aligned with the single-process requirement.

1) Runtime: Node.js using ESM modules.

2) Telegram framework: grammY for bot commands, launch buttons, update handling, and Telegram integration. TELEGRAMBOTTOKEN is loaded from environment variables.

3) Mini App: Telegram Mini App WebView running a modern browser-based 3D game frontend. The frontend should use a mobile-friendly 3D rendering library or direct browser graphics APIs, with careful asset compression and frame-rate optimization.

4) Backend HTTP layer: the same Node.js service serves Mini App files and exposes game APIs for initialization, profile retrieval, leaderboard retrieval, and score submission.

5) Persistence: MongoDB for users, game runs, leaderboards, coin balances, and audit data. Simple in-memory maps may be used for optional short-lived rate limiting or cooldowns, understanding that they reset when the process restarts.

6) File and image storage: game assets can be served from simple filesystem storage or generic object storage. No separate database is required for assets.

7) Deployment: a single Node.js server in the cloud with HTTPS support. The service should expose Telegram webhook handling, Mini App routes, API routes, and health checks from the same process.

8) Configuration: environment variables for bot token, public Mini App URL, MongoDB connection string, runtime environment, logging level, and score validation thresholds.

9) Observability: structured application logs, request ids, startup checks, health endpoint, and error reporting through logs. Keep operational dependencies minimal and avoid external queues or additional infrastructure.

Non-functional requirements:
1) Performance: the Mini App should target smooth gameplay on modern mobile devices. Keep initial load small, compress 3D assets, avoid excessive draw calls, reuse objects during gameplay, and avoid memory growth across restarts.

2) Reliability: the game loop should not depend on continuous network access. Backend calls happen at initialization, leaderboard loading, and score submission. Bot command handling and Mini App APIs should fail gracefully with user-friendly messages.

3) Security: validate Telegram Mini App init data on the server before trusting identity. Never accept user ids directly from the client as proof of identity. Apply input validation, output sanitization, request size limits, duplicate run protection, and score plausibility checks.

4) Privacy: store only the Telegram identity fields needed for gameplay and leaderboards. Provide a privacy command explaining what is stored. Consider a user status field for deleted, banned, or hidden-from-leaderboard accounts.

5) Abuse prevention: rate limit score submissions per user in memory, reject impossible scores, cap coins per run, track suspicious client versions, and allow manual invalidation of leaderboard entries through data updates.

6) Maintainability: separate concerns between bot handlers, Mini App API handlers, game domain services, validation rules, persistence adapters, and static asset delivery. Keep gameplay constants versioned so score validation can match the client version.

7) Compatibility: test inside Telegram Mini App WebView on common mobile screen sizes and both touch interaction styles: drag movement and virtual joystick. Handle safe areas, orientation changes, app backgrounding, and WebView focus loss.

8) Scalability within constraints: MongoDB indexes should support leaderboard reads and user lookups efficiently. Since the service is single-process, long-running work should be avoided in request handlers, and the game client should do all rendering and physics locally.

9) Release management: use appVersion and gameRulesVersion in client initialization and score submission. This allows the backend to validate old runs correctly during gradual frontend updates.

Bot commands:
• start — Welcomes the user, explains Orbit Dash briefly, and presents the main Play Orbit Dash button that opens the Telegram Mini App. (usage: /start) [onboarding]
• play — Opens the Orbit Dash Mini App directly. This is the fastest path for returning players who want to start a new run. (usage: /play) [game]
• leaderboard — Shows a compact leaderboard summary in chat and provides a button to open the full leaderboard inside the Mini App. (usage: /leaderboard) [competition]
• profile — Shows the player’s best score, total runs, total coins collected, current coin balance, and recent rank summary. (usage: /profile) [account]
• help — Explains the controls, objective, scoring rules, coins, asteroids, restart behavior, and how leaderboard rankings work. (usage: /help) [support]
• settings — Provides access to simple player preferences such as sound, haptics if supported, control style, and leaderboard display visibility. (usage: /settings) [account]
• privacy — Explains what Telegram profile data and gameplay data are stored, why they are needed, and how they are used for scores and leaderboards. (usage: /privacy) [support]

DOCS.md.content:
# Orbit Dash Documentation

Orbit Dash is a Telegram-only Mini App game. Players pilot a small spaceship through a space runner course, dodge asteroids, collect coins, and survive as long as possible.

The project runs as a single Node.js service. It starts a grammY Telegram bot and hosts the Mini App web game from the same process at `/app`.

## Public commands

### /start

Usage: `/start`

Welcomes the user to Orbit Dash and shows a Telegram Web App button that opens the game.

### /help

Usage: `/help`

Explains how to play: move the spaceship with touch controls, dodge asteroids, collect coins, survive longer for more score, and restart after game over.

## Environment variables

### TELEGRAM_BOT_TOKEN

Required. The Telegram bot token from BotFather. The service exits with a clear message if this is missing.

### WEBAPP_URL

Optional. The public URL that Telegram should open for the game, normally ending in `/app`.

Example:

text
https://your-service.onrender.com/app


If it is missing, the app falls back to `http://localhost:<PORT>/app` for local development. This keeps startup safe, but Telegram mobile users need a public HTTPS URL.

### PORT

Optional. HTTP port for the single Node service. Defaults to `3000`.

## Local run

Install root dependencies and web app dependencies:

bash
npm run install:root
npm run install:webapp


Build the Mini App:

bash
npm run build:webapp


Start the service:

bash
npm start


Open the local game at:

text
http://localhost:3000/app


## Telegram Mini App URL setup

For Telegram mobile testing, deploy the service to a public HTTPS URL and set:

text
WEBAPP_URL=https://your-public-host/app


Then send `/start` to the bot and tap Play Orbit Dash.

## Gameplay

The game includes a start screen, live game screen, and game over screen.

During a run:

1. Move the spaceship with drag or the on-screen controls.
2. Avoid asteroids. Hitting one ends the run.
3. Collect coins for bonus points.
4. Survive longer to increase score and difficulty.
5. Press Restart after game over to reset the run without leaving Telegram.

Desktop testing supports arrow keys and WASD.

## Deployment notes

Use one Node web service. Do not run a separate worker. The root `start` script runs the HTTP server and the Telegram bot together.

Recommended Render settings:

Build command:

bash
npm run build


Start command:

bash
npm start


Required environment variable:

text
TELEGRAM_BOT_TOKEN


Recommended environment variable:

text
WEBAPP_URL=https://your-service.onrender.com/app


## Troubleshooting

If the service exits, check that `TELEGRAM_BOT_TOKEN` is set.

If Telegram says the Web App cannot open, confirm `WEBAPP_URL` is public, HTTPS, and ends with `/app`.

If the game shows a WebGL error, update Telegram, try a newer mobile device, or test in a browser that supports WebGL.

If the web page says the app is not built, run `npm run build:webapp` before `npm start`.

Logs print only safe booleans such as whether `TELEGRAM_BOT_TOKEN` and `WEBAPP_URL` are set. Secrets are never printed.

README.md.content:
# Orbit Dash

Orbit Dash is a Telegram Mini App game served by one Node.js service. The same process runs the grammY Telegram bot, hosts the React and Phaser web game at `/app`, and exposes a small health endpoint.

## Features

- Telegram bot commands: `/start` and `/help`
- Telegram Web App button that opens Orbit Dash
- Mobile-first Telegram WebView UI
- Phaser 3 arcade loop with spaceship movement, asteroids, coins, score, survival timer, difficulty progression, and restart flow
- Local high score and total coin persistence in the browser
- Production-safe startup logs that only print environment booleans

## Setup

1. Install dependencies:

bash
npm run install:root
npm run install:webapp


2. Copy `.env.sample` to `.env` and set `TELEGRAM_BOT_TOKEN`.

3. Build the web app:

bash
npm run build:webapp


4. Start the single service:

bash
npm start


The game is served at `http://localhost:3000/app` by default.

## Environment variables

- `TELEGRAM_BOT_TOKEN`: required. Telegram bot token from BotFather.
- `WEBAPP_URL`: optional but recommended. Full public URL to the game, for example `https://your-service.onrender.com/app`.
- `PORT`: optional. HTTP port for the Node service. Defaults to `3000`.

## Commands

- `/start`: welcomes the player and shows the Play Orbit Dash Web App button.
- `/help`: explains the objective, touch controls, scoring, coins, asteroids, and restart flow.

## Deployment

Deploy as one Render web service or similar Node host.

Build command:

bash
npm run build


Start command:

bash
npm start


Set `TELEGRAM_BOT_TOKEN`. Set `WEBAPP_URL` to your deployed `/app` URL when available.

## Troubleshooting

If the bot exits at startup, confirm `TELEGRAM_BOT_TOKEN` is set. Logs only show whether the token is present, never the token value.

If the button opens a local URL inside Telegram, set `WEBAPP_URL` to a public HTTPS URL ending in `/app`.

If the game shows a WebGL error, update Telegram, try another device, or confirm the WebView supports WebGL.

state.planMarkdown:
Bot: Orbit Dash Telegram Mini App Production Plan

Summary:
Orbit Dash is a Telegram Mini App game where players pilot a small spaceship through a 3D space tunnel, dodge asteroids, collect coins, and compete for survival time and high scores.

The production system consists of a single Node.js ESM service using grammY for Telegram bot interactions, serving the Mini App frontend, exposing lightweight game APIs, validating Telegram Mini App identity data, and persisting player progress and scores in MongoDB. The game itself runs inside the Telegram Mini App WebView and is optimized for mobile touch controls, fast loading, and stable frame rates.

Product goals:
Orbit Dash should feel like a lightweight arcade game that opens instantly from Telegram and is simple to understand within a few seconds.

1) Core gameplay: the player controls a spaceship moving left, right, up, and down while the world scrolls forward. Asteroids appear as obstacles, coins appear as collectibles, and survival time increases difficulty.

2) Required screens: the Mini App includes a start screen, gameplay screen, pause or focus-loss handling, game over screen, score summary, coin summary, and restart button.

3) Telegram-native experience: the bot provides a clear entry point through commands and buttons, while the Mini App uses Telegram user identity for profiles, leaderboard entries, and score submission.

4) Competitive loop: players can replay quickly, improve their score, collect coins, and compare scores through daily, weekly, and all-time leaderboards.

5) Production readiness: the system should include identity validation, score plausibility checks, rate limiting, error handling, privacy-aware data storage, asset optimization, and operational visibility through structured logs and basic health checks.

High-level architecture:
The system runs as one Node.js service/process. It has three responsibilities in the same service: Telegram bot handling through grammY, Mini App delivery, and game backend APIs.

1) Telegram bot layer: grammY receives updates from Telegram and handles commands such as start, play, leaderboard, profile, help, and privacy. The bot sends Mini App launch buttons that open Orbit Dash inside Telegram WebView. The Telegram bot token is read from the TELEGRAMBOTTOKEN environment variable.

2) Mini App frontend: the browser game is delivered as static frontend assets from the Node.js service or from simple filesystem storage. It contains the start screen, 3D renderer, touch controls, collision detection, scoring HUD, game over screen, and restart loop. The rendering and gameplay loop run locally in the WebView to avoid network dependency during active play.

3) Backend API layer: the Mini App calls backend endpoints to initialize the user session, fetch player profile, fetch leaderboard data, submit game results, and update non-sensitive preferences. Every Mini App API request that depends on identity must validate Telegram Mini App init data server-side before trusting the Telegram user id.

4) Persistence layer: MongoDB stores users, game runs, leaderboard records, coin balances, and audit metadata. In-memory maps may be used only for optional short-lived concerns such as per-process rate limits, replay submission cooldowns, and temporary health counters.

5) Game client structure: the game loop separates rendering, input, spawning, physics, collision, scoring, and UI state. The client should use deterministic limits for maximum movement speed, asteroid spawn rate, coin spawn rate, and score growth so the backend can reject impossible submissions.

6) Asset handling: spaceship, asteroid, coin, background, sound, and texture assets are compressed and versioned. They may be served from simple filesystem storage or generic object storage. The first playable experience should load with a small critical asset set, with optional assets loaded after the start screen.

7) Deployment shape: a single Node.js server in the cloud exposes HTTPS endpoints for Telegram webhooks, Mini App pages, static assets, health checks, and game APIs. The service should support graceful shutdown, environment-based configuration, and clear separation between public Mini App routes and internal operational routes.

Data model:
MongoDB is the primary persistence store. The schema should remain simple and optimized for user lookup, recent runs, and leaderboard queries.

1) users: stores Telegram user identity and gameplay profile. Suggested fields include telegramUserId, username, firstName, languageCode, createdAt, lastSeenAt, totalRuns, bestScore, bestSurvivalMs, totalCoinsCollected, spendableCoins, settings, and status.

2) gameRuns: stores each completed attempt. Suggested fields include runId, telegramUserId, startedAt, endedAt, durationMs, score, coinsCollected, asteroidsDodged, maxDifficultyLevel, clientVersion, validationStatus, rejectionReason, deviceHints, and createdAt.

3) leaderboardEntries: stores denormalized best results for fast leaderboard display. Suggested fields include scope, periodKey, telegramUserId, displayName, score, survivalMs, coinsCollected, runId, rankUpdatedAt, and createdAt. Scopes can include daily, weekly, and allTime.

4) coinLedger: optional but recommended if coins will later unlock skins or upgrades. Suggested fields include ledgerId, telegramUserId, amount, reason, sourceRunId, balanceAfter, createdAt, and metadata. For a simple first release, coin totals can be stored directly on users and runs, but a ledger is safer for future economy features.

5) userSessions or launchEvents: optional analytics collection for Mini App opens. Suggested fields include telegramUserId, launchAt, platform, appVersion, referrerCommand, and initValidationStatus.

6) adminEvents: optional collection for moderation or operational actions, such as resetting suspicious scores, banning abusive users, or changing leaderboard visibility.

Important indexes include unique telegramUserId on users, telegramUserId plus createdAt on gameRuns, scope plus periodKey plus score descending on leaderboardEntries, and telegramUserId plus scope plus periodKey on leaderboardEntries.

Key flows:
1) First launch flow: the user sends /start or taps a shared bot link. The bot replies with a short explanation of Orbit Dash and a Play Orbit Dash button. When tapped, Telegram opens the Mini App WebView. The Mini App sends Telegram init data to the backend, the backend validates it, creates or updates the user, and returns profile data plus current leaderboard summary.

2) Start screen flow: the Mini App shows the game title, best score, total coins, a Play button, a leaderboard shortcut, and basic control instructions. Assets needed for the first run are preloaded before enabling Play.

3) Gameplay flow: after tapping Play, the spaceship appears in a 3D space lane. The player uses mobile touch controls, such as drag-to-move or virtual joystick movement. Asteroids spawn ahead and move toward the player. Coins spawn in reachable paths. The score increases with survival time, coins collected, and asteroids dodged. Difficulty increases gradually through speed, obstacle density, and coin placement risk.

4) Collision and collection flow: the client detects spaceship collisions with asteroids and coins. Asteroid collision ends the run. Coin collision increments the coin counter and may provide immediate feedback through animation and sound. The HUD continuously displays score, coins, and survival time.

5) Game over flow: when a collision occurs, the client stops the game loop, shows final score, survival time, coins collected, and whether a new personal best was achieved. The client submits the run result to the backend. The backend validates plausibility, stores the run, updates the user profile, updates leaderboard entries if eligible, and returns the accepted result plus rank information.

6) Restart flow: the Restart button resets local game state without leaving the Mini App. The user can replay immediately. The backend does not need to create a server-side run at the moment gameplay starts unless anti-abuse requirements increase; for launch, validating submitted completed runs is sufficient.

7) Leaderboard flow: users can open leaderboards from the bot command or Mini App. The backend returns top daily, weekly, and all-time scores, plus the current user’s rank if available. Display names should be derived from Telegram profile data but sanitized and length-limited.

8) Anti-cheat flow: the backend rejects impossible run submissions based on duration, maximum theoretical score, maximum coin count, movement limits, game version, duplicate run ids, and submission frequency. Suspicious but not impossible runs can be stored with a flagged validation status and excluded from leaderboards pending review.

9) Returning user flow: when a known player opens the Mini App, the backend updates lastSeenAt and returns saved profile data, best score, coin balance, and latest leaderboard rank. The game should remain playable even if leaderboard fetching fails, but score submission requires a valid backend response.

10) Error flow: if Mini App identity validation fails, the game shows a friendly message asking the user to reopen from Telegram. If the backend is temporarily unavailable, the client should allow a local run but clearly indicate that leaderboard submission may not be saved.

Tech stack:
The production stack is intentionally simple and aligned with the single-process requirement.

1) Runtime: Node.js using ESM modules.

2) Telegram framework: grammY for bot commands, launch buttons, update handling, and Telegram integration. TELEGRAMBOTTOKEN is loaded from environment variables.

3) Mini App: Telegram Mini App WebView running a modern browser-based 3D game frontend. The frontend should use a mobile-friendly 3D rendering library or direct browser graphics APIs, with careful asset compression and frame-rate optimization.

4) Backend HTTP layer: the same Node.js service serves Mini App files and exposes game APIs for initialization, profile retrieval, leaderboard retrieval, and score submission.

5) Persistence: MongoDB for users, game runs, leaderboards, coin balances, and audit data. Simple in-memory maps may be used for optional short-lived rate limiting or cooldowns, understanding that they reset when the process restarts.

6) File and image storage: game assets can be served from simple filesystem storage or generic object storage. No separate database is required for assets.

7) Deployment: a single Node.js server in the cloud with HTTPS support. The service should expose Telegram webhook handling, Mini App routes, API routes, and health checks from the same process.

8) Configuration: environment variables for bot token, public Mini App URL, MongoDB connection string, runtime environment, logging level, and score validation thresholds.

9) Observability: structured application logs, request ids, startup checks, health endpoint, and error reporting through logs. Keep operational dependencies minimal and avoid external queues or additional infrastructure.

Non-functional requirements:
1) Performance: the Mini App should target smooth gameplay on modern mobile devices. Keep initial load small, compress 3D assets, avoid excessive draw calls, reuse objects during gameplay, and avoid memory growth across restarts.

2) Reliability: the game loop should not depend on continuous network access. Backend calls happen at initialization, leaderboard loading, and score submission. Bot command handling and Mini App APIs should fail gracefully with user-friendly messages.

3) Security: validate Telegram Mini App init data on the server before trusting identity. Never accept user ids directly from the client as proof of identity. Apply input validation, output sanitization, request size limits, duplicate run protection, and score plausibility checks.

4) Privacy: store only the Telegram identity fields needed for gameplay and leaderboards. Provide a privacy command explaining what is stored. Consider a user status field for deleted, banned, or hidden-from-leaderboard accounts.

5) Abuse prevention: rate limit score submissions per user in memory, reject impossible scores, cap coins per run, track suspicious client versions, and allow manual invalidation of leaderboard entries through data updates.

6) Maintainability: separate concerns between bot handlers, Mini App API handlers, game domain services, validation rules, persistence adapters, and static asset delivery. Keep gameplay constants versioned so score validation can match the client version.

7) Compatibility: test inside Telegram Mini App WebView on common mobile screen sizes and both touch interaction styles: drag movement and virtual joystick. Handle safe areas, orientation changes, app backgrounding, and WebView focus loss.

8) Scalability within constraints: MongoDB indexes should support leaderboard reads and user lookups efficiently. Since the service is single-process, long-running work should be avoided in request handlers, and the game client should do all rendering and physics locally.

9) Release management: use appVersion and gameRulesVersion in client initialization and score submission. This allows the backend to validate old runs correctly during gradual frontend updates.

Bot commands:
• start — Welcomes the user, explains Orbit Dash briefly, and presents the main Play Orbit Dash button that opens the Telegram Mini App. (usage: /start) [onboarding]
• play — Opens the Orbit Dash Mini App directly. This is the fastest path for returning players who want to start a new run. (usage: /play) [game]
• leaderboard — Shows a compact leaderboard summary in chat and provides a button to open the full leaderboard inside the Mini App. (usage: /leaderboard) [competition]
• profile — Shows the player’s best score, total runs, total coins collected, current coin balance, and recent rank summary. (usage: /profile) [account]
• help — Explains the controls, objective, scoring rules, coins, asteroids, restart behavior, and how leaderboard rankings work. (usage: /help) [support]
• settings — Provides access to simple player preferences such as sound, haptics if supported, control style, and leaderboard display visibility. (usage: /settings) [account]
• privacy — Explains what Telegram profile data and gameplay data are stored, why they are needed, and how they are used for scores and leaderboards. (usage: /privacy) [support]
